title: Chapter 5 - GitHub Remotes: Clone, Push, Pull
description: Check your understanding of remotes and syncing with GitHub.

options.label: Copies a remote repository to your computer

explanations.overview: Cloning downloads a repository so you can work on it locally.

byOptionId.a: Incorrect. Clone does not delete repositories.
byOptionId.b: Incorrect. Clone does not create commit messages.
byOptionId.c: Incorrect. Clone does not rename branches.
byOptionId.d: Correct. Clone copies a remote repo to your computer.

questions.prompt: In Git, what is `origin` usually used for?

options.label: A special branch that cannot be changed
A Git command that stages files
The default nickname for the main remote

explanations.overview: `origin` is typically the name given to the remote URL when you clone a repo.

byOptionId.b: Incorrect. `origin` is not a Git command.
byOptionId.c: Correct. `origin` is the common default remote name.
byOptionId.d: Incorrect. It is not a file extension.

options.label: Sends your local commits to the remote
Turns your code into a website

explanations.overview: Push uploads your commits to the remote repository.

byOptionId.a: Incorrect. Downloading changes is usually `git pull` or `git fetch`.
byOptionId.b: Correct. Push sends your commits to the remote.
byOptionId.c: Incorrect. `.gitignore` is a file you create/edit manually.
byOptionId.d: Incorrect. Git does not publish websites by itself.

options.label: Brings remote commits into your local branch
Forces everyone to use your code

explanations.overview: Pull updates your local branch with commits from the remote.

byOptionId.a: Correct. Pull brings remote work to your computer.
byOptionId.b: Incorrect. Deleting commits is a different set of commands.
byOptionId.c: Incorrect. Collaboration requires pushing and merging via branches/PRs.
byOptionId.d: Incorrect. Creating a repo is separate from pulling.
